# Study Design Framework

The study design converts a confirmed research spine into an evidence-producing plan.

## Design components

| Component | Required Question |
|---|---|
| Research question | What exactly will be answered? |
| Unit of analysis | What object is measured, simulated, observed, or compared? |
| Data/material | What evidence source is used? |
| Method/approach | What is done to the evidence source? |
| Baseline/control | What is the comparison? |
| Evaluation criterion | What counts as support? |
| Robustness check | Would the claim survive plausible perturbations? |
| Negative/failure case | Where should the claim not hold? |
| Interpretation boundary | What cannot be concluded? |

## Different evidence modes

### Computational

Require dataset provenance, preprocessing, split logic, baseline models, metrics, ablations, robustness checks, and leakage checks.

### Experimental

Require sample/material description, controls, randomization or blocking when applicable, measurement protocol, uncertainty, replication, exclusion rules, and safety constraints.

### Theoretical

Require assumptions, definitions, derivation/proof logic, limiting cases, examples or counterexamples, and relation to known results.

### Qualitative or design-oriented

Require sampling rationale, coding/interpretive framework, triangulation, saturation or coverage logic, and clear distinction between observation and interpretation.

## Output rule

No design step should be included only because it is conventional. Every step must support a claim, rule out an alternative explanation, or establish scope.
